questions.prompt: Linux permissions show three little `rwx` groups. Who does each group apply to, and what does execute mean on a directory?

answers.text: Those mode bits apply to filesystem objects broadly, not only to regular executable files.

feedback.legit: Mode bits apply broadly to filesystem objects. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: Mode bits apply broadly to filesystem objects. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: For a directory, execute controls traversal and access through it. You are not 'running' the directory.

feedback.legit: Execute permission on a directory controls traversal rather than running the directory. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: Execute permission on a directory controls traversal rather than running the directory. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: The three groups belong to the owning user, the owning group, and everyone else.

feedback.legit: They are grouped for the owning user, owning group, and others. Your call matches the underlying rule.
feedback.sus: They are grouped for the owning user, owning group, and others. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

questions.prompt: A program file can launch more than once, so what makes each running Linux process its own thing?

answers.text: Each process gets an identifier and its own virtual address-space context among its runtime resources.

feedback.legit: Each process has an identifier and a virtual address space. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: Each process has an identifier and a virtual address space. The claim is supported rather than suspicious.

answers.text: Think of it as a running instance of a program, complete with execution state and resources managed by the kernel.

feedback.legit: A running program instance with execution state and resources. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: A running program instance with execution state and resources. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

answers.text: A process is just the executable file on disk, and Linux permits only one running instance of that file.

feedback.legit: The same executable can be used by many processes. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: The same executable can be used by many processes. You spotted the misleading part.

questions.prompt: In `a | b`, what stream leaves command `a`, and where exactly does the shell connect it on `b`?

answers.text: The shell connects `a`'s standard output to `b`'s standard input.

feedback.legit: The standard output of `a` to the standard input of `b`. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: The standard output of `a` to the standard input of `b`. This is a Legit statement, not a trap.

answers.text: It automatically joins the filesystems of the machines running the two commands, even across a network.

feedback.legit: A shell pipe carries a byte stream between local processes unless other tools add networking. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: A shell pipe carries a byte stream between local processes unless other tools add networking. You flagged exactly what was wrong.

answers.text: The pipe preserves whatever record boundaries the first application used, so the second receives intact messages.

feedback.legit: A normal pipe is a byte stream; applications must define their own framing when needed. The original reply should be marked Sus.
feedback.sus: A normal pipe is a byte stream; applications must define their own framing when needed. Sus was the correct call here.

questions.prompt: After a process calls `fork()`, what exists on the other side of that call, and is all memory copied immediately?

answers.text: The child is a separate process with its own PID, even though it begins from the parent's state.

feedback.legit: The child is a separate process with its own PID. You were right to trust this reply.
feedback.sus: The child is a separate process with its own PID. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: Copy-on-write commonly lets them share physical pages until one process modifies a page.

feedback.legit: Copy-on-write commonly delays copying memory pages until one process writes. Your Legit vote was right.
feedback.sus: Copy-on-write commonly delays copying memory pages until one process writes. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: You get a child process initially based on the caller. Both processes continue from the return point of `fork()`.

feedback.legit: A child process initially based on the calling process. Your Legit judgment holds up.
feedback.sus: A child process initially based on the calling process. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

questions.prompt: Unix signals are called asynchronous notifications. How reliable are they as a message queue, and can every signal be caught?

answers.text: Some signals can be handled or blocked, but `SIGKILL` cannot be caught or ignored.

feedback.legit: Some signals can be handled or blocked, while SIGKILL cannot be caught. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: Some signals can be handled or blocked, while SIGKILL cannot be caught. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: A signal is an asynchronous notification delivered to a process or, in some cases, a particular thread.

feedback.legit: An asynchronous notification delivered to a process or thread. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: An asynchronous notification delivered to a process or thread. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: Signals form an unlimited reliable queue. Repeated identical notifications are always preserved one by one.

feedback.legit: Standard signals can coalesce and are not an unlimited message queue. The original reply should be marked Sus.
feedback.sus: Standard signals can coalesce and are not an unlimited message queue. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

questions.prompt: What does a symbolic link store, and why can it cross filesystems or point at something that does not exist yet?

answers.text: It stores a path that will be resolved when the link is followed.

feedback.legit: A symbolic link stores a path to another filesystem object. That is a solid Legit pick.
feedback.sus: A symbolic link stores a path to another filesystem object. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: A symlink and its target must share a filesystem because they reference the same inode.

feedback.legit: Symbolic links can point across filesystem boundaries. The original reply should be marked Sus.
feedback.sus: Symbolic links can point across filesystem boundaries. Good read: the original claim was flawed.

answers.text: Linux refuses to create a symbolic link until the target path already exists and is readable.

feedback.legit: A symbolic link can be created for a nonexistent path and will then be dangling. The Legit label misses the flaw here.
feedback.sus: A symbolic link can be created for a nonexistent path and will then be dangling. Your judgment caught the technical error.

questions.prompt: If two directory entries are hard links to the same file, what are they both pointing at underneath?

answers.text: They store a target pathname and follow that name again on every access, just like symbolic links.

feedback.legit: That describes a symbolic link; a hard link is another name for the inode. The original claim needed a Sus vote.
feedback.sus: That describes a symbolic link; a hard link is another name for the inode. Good read: the original claim was flawed.

answers.text: Removing one name does not remove the data while another hard link still references it.

feedback.legit: Removing one hard link does not remove the data while another link remains. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: Removing one hard link does not remove the data while another link remains. The rule confirms the reply instead of refuting it.

answers.text: Both directory entries refer to the same inode and therefore the same underlying file data.

feedback.legit: The same inode and underlying file data as another directory entry. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: The same inode and underlying file data as another directory entry. The evidence supports the original reply.

questions.prompt: A server shows load average `1.0`. Why can't I translate that directly into '100% CPU'?

answers.text: The three reported numbers summarize load over different windows, commonly one, five, and fifteen minutes.

feedback.legit: The three values summarize different time windows. Your call matches the underlying rule.
feedback.sus: The three values summarize different time windows. The evidence supports the original reply.

answers.text: `1.0` always equals 100 percent CPU usage, whether the machine has one core or sixty-four.

feedback.legit: Load average counts runnable and certain uninterruptible tasks and must be interpreted with CPU count. That makes the original claim unsafe to label Legit.
feedback.sus: Load average counts runnable and certain uninterruptible tasks and must be interpreted with CPU count. You spotted the misleading part.

answers.text: Tasks waiting on certain I/O can contribute to load average, so it is not just a CPU-utilization meter.

feedback.legit: I/O-waiting tasks can contribute to load average. You were right to trust this reply.
feedback.sus: I/O-waiting tasks can contribute to load average. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

questions.prompt: Containers use Linux control groups, but what are cgroups actually controlling or measuring?

answers.text: They group processes for resource accounting, allocation, and limits such as CPU or memory controls.

feedback.legit: Resource accounting and limits for groups of processes. You were right to trust this reply.
feedback.sus: Resource accounting and limits for groups of processes. This is a Legit statement, not a trap.

answers.text: Cgroups replace filesystem ownership and permission checks, so container files no longer need Unix modes.

feedback.legit: Cgroups manage resources, while permissions remain a separate security control. The original reply is what needed flagging.
feedback.sus: Cgroups manage resources, while permissions remain a separate security control. Your judgment caught the technical error.

answers.text: Separate PID and network views come from namespaces. Cgroups provide those isolated views instead.

feedback.legit: Namespaces isolate resource views; cgroups primarily account for and control resource usage. The original reply was the misconception in the thread.
feedback.sus: Namespaces isolate resource views; cgroups primarily account for and control resource usage. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

questions.prompt: When people say a container has an 'isolated view' of Linux, which kernel feature creates those separate views?

answers.text: Namespaces encrypt all communication between processes automatically. Isolation is cryptographic by default.

feedback.legit: Namespaces isolate views; encryption is a separate feature. The original reply is what needed flagging.
feedback.sus: Namespaces isolate views; encryption is a separate feature. The evidence supports your Sus vote.

answers.text: Containers combine several namespace types with cgroups, capabilities, and other controls rather than relying on one switch.

feedback.legit: Containers combine multiple namespace types with other controls. You were right to trust this reply.
feedback.sus: Containers combine multiple namespace types with other controls. The evidence supports the original reply.

answers.text: Namespaces isolate selected views such as process IDs, mount points, host names, users, or network stacks.

feedback.legit: Selected views of system resources such as process IDs, mounts, or networks. Your Legit vote was right.
feedback.sus: Selected views of system resources such as process IDs, mounts, or networks. The claim is supported rather than suspicious.

questions.prompt: Why does `epoll` scale better than repeatedly scanning thousands of file descriptors for readiness?

answers.text: With edge-triggered notification, you need to drain the resource carefully or you can wait forever for an edge that already happened.

feedback.legit: Edge-triggered use requires draining readiness carefully to avoid missed progress. Your call matches the underlying rule.
feedback.sus: Edge-triggered use requires draining readiness carefully to avoid missed progress. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

answers.text: The kernel can report ready descriptors without the caller rescanning the entire descriptor set on every wait.

feedback.legit: It can report readiness changes without scanning every descriptor on each wait. You classified that correctly as Legit.
feedback.sus: It can report readiness changes without scanning every descriptor on each wait. This one should stay marked Legit.

answers.text: It converts every blocking file operation into CPU-parallel work. Readiness and parallel execution are the same thing.

feedback.legit: Epoll reports readiness; the application still performs the I/O. The original statement is materially misleading.
feedback.sus: Epoll reports readiness; the application still performs the I/O. That was correctly classified as Sus.

questions.prompt: When Linux cannot satisfy memory demand, what can the OOM path do, and what myths about its victim choice should I avoid?

answers.text: An out-of-memory event can happen only when swap is completely disabled. With swap configured, memory is unlimited.

feedback.legit: OOM can occur with or without swap when the relevant memory domain cannot satisfy an allocation. That distinction makes the original answer Sus.
feedback.sus: OOM can occur with or without swap when the relevant memory domain cannot satisfy an allocation. Your Sus vote caught the problem.

answers.text: The kernel responds by adding physical RAM automatically. Applications never need to handle memory exhaustion.

feedback.legit: Memory is finite; reclaim, swap, and termination are possible responses. The error puts the original reply under Sus.
feedback.sus: Memory is finite; reclaim, swap, and termination are possible responses. You were right not to trust that reply.

answers.text: The OOM killer always selects whichever process currently uses the most memory. Its scoring has no other inputs.

feedback.legit: The kernel uses an OOM scoring policy, so raw memory use alone does not determine the victim. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: The kernel uses an OOM scoring policy, so raw memory use alone does not determine the victim. The evidence supports your Sus vote.
